metrics.narrative: Buyer articulated qualitative pain around poor ERP data quality and inability to execute category management, but no quantified business case. Brandon Wood stated: 'It's hard to put yourself in a good place from a negotiation standpoint or even really identify what your opportunities are for consolidation and synergies without having good data. The data is not that good, or it would just take us too long to sift through all of it and categorize it properly' (discovery call 2026-04-23). He described the pain owner as the category management team (category leads, formerly contract specialists) who 'don't really have the time to do it the way it traditionally should be done' due to manual categorization burden after a 2024 Infor ERP implementation that replaced paper requisitions but produced weak reporting. No quantified savings target, ROI goal, headcount efficiency, or cycle-time reduction was stated. Buyer mentioned interest in AI to 'rationalize data quicker' and 'identify opportunities for consolidation' but did not tie it to a dollar figure or timeline pressure beyond the FY27 budget constraint (flat budget, no new spend). Gap: no quantified value case or compelling financial event anchoring the deal. Next move is to co-build a savings model (procurement spend consolidation, contract compliance lift, Maverick spend reduction) with Finance and Procurement leads and tie it to a measurable ROI within 12 months.

champion.narrative: Champion is identified as Brandon Wood (VP Procurement), but strength is weak-to-developing with cooling risk. Brandon showed genuine interest on the discovery call (asked detailed questions, screen-shared VIA's internal category taxonomy, acknowledged the fit, and committed to internal circulation and follow-up in 2-3 weeks). He demonstrated access to the decision process (he named the VPs who must sign off and the CEO budget mandate) and acknowledged Zycus could address the pain. However, he has not acted as an advocate: no follow-up in 64 days since the call (the 2-3 week commitment is now 5-6 weeks overdue), no inbound contact, no evidence he circulated the materials internally or drove the evaluation forward, no introduction to IT/Finance VPs, no request for a follow-up meeting. He also explicitly stated 'I'm in no way the start and end of this decision' and 'we're meeting with a couple other AI firms', signaling he is one voice among many and not a sole driver. His engagement was passive-positive (interested and responsive during the call) but not active-advocacy (no internal selling, no pulling Zycus deeper, no pushing back on competitors for us, no shared internal docs or eval scorecard). Brian Colbert (Director Procurement) was on the call and asked technical questions about Infor integration and approval workflows, indicating he is an influencer/user, but he has no budget authority or exec access (reports to Brandon). No other champions identified: no IT VP, no CFO, no procurement executive above Brandon. Champion strength is developing but now cooling due to 64-day silence. At-risk flag: yes — the deal is stalled, Brandon has not re-engaged, and competing vendors are in the mix. Next move: re-engage Brandon with a light-touch value-add (send the Spend Analysis product demo with VIA's taxonomy mocked up as he requested, plus a one-page ROI framework for cross-divisional justification) and ask for a 15-minute check-in to understand where the internal evaluation landed and whether the 2-3 week follow-up can now happen. If no reply in 10 business days, escalate to Michael McCarthy (deal owner's manager) for an exec-to-exec touch with Brandon or the CFO to revive the deal.

SF Competitors__c field: blank (hygiene gap — b2g and the unnamed AI vendors should be logged)
competition.narrative: VIA is actively evaluating multiple vendors and has already deployed a competing platform. Primary active threat: b2g eSourcing platform is live in production as of the week before the discovery call ('We're actually in the middle of implementing the eSourcing platform from a company called b2g. We actually just released our first live event last week' per Brandon Wood, discovery call 2026-04-23). This is a high-threat displacement risk: b2g owns the eSourcing module, which is part of Zycus's full Source-to-Pay offering, and VIA has already committed budget and implementation effort to it. Brandon's tone was matter-of-fact (not looking to replace b2g immediately), and he acknowledged Zycus eSourcing overlaps with what b2g does, signaling Zycus would need to displace or integrate. Secondary active threat: 'a couple other AI firms that do not exactly what you guys do but something similar' (Brandon Wood, discovery call 2026-04-23). No vendor names given, no details on what those firms offer, no indication of eval stage or timeline. Likely competitors in the procurement-AI / spend-analytics space (e.g., Coupa, Jaggaer, Ivalua, or niche AI analytics vendors), but unconfirmed. Threat level medium (multi-vendor eval is active, but no named finalist yet). Incumbent baseline: Infor ERP (contracts and requisitions live in Infor, implemented 2024, replacing paper processes). Infor is the system of record and integration anchor, not a competitor to Zycus, but it creates inertia (buyer asked multiple questions about Infor integration, signaling IT will require seamless ERP connectivity as a gate). Do-nothing risk: low to medium. Brandon acknowledged the pain is real ('we can't execute category management without good data'), but the pain is not urgent (no crisis, no forcing date), and the FY27 budget is flat with 'not a whole lot of meat on the bone'. If the business case doesn't justify cross-divisional ROI, VIA can defer the purchase and live with manual category management for another year. Competitive wedge: Zycus's AI-powered Spend Analysis with custom taxonomy fit is the unique value prop Brandon asked for ('take our data and make it fit into these boxes'), and full Source-to-Pay integration (eSourcing + Contract + eProcurement + Supplier Management) offers a path to displace b2g long-term if VIA wants to consolidate vendors. The wedge is cross-divisional ROI (Procurement + Finance + IT + Operations efficiency) and FTA compliance built into the platform. Activation: co-build a Spend Analysis POC using VIA's actual category taxonomy (construction services breakdown Brandon screen-shared) and show categorized spend output in 2-4 weeks; position it as a low-cost Phase 1 entry point ($70-140k/year standalone) with option to expand to full Source-to-Pay in FY28+ as budget opens, framing Zycus as the long-term single-platform play vs. a patchwork of b2g + Infor + manual processes.

sources: Discovery call 2026-04-23, Brandon Wood detailed description of ERP data quality pain and category management execution gap
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and clearly articulated by the buyer, owned by the category management team (category leads, formerly contract specialists reporting to Brandon Wood). Brandon stated: 'The problem is they don't really have the time to do it the way it traditionally should be done. The data output is not very robust because our ERP system has only been implemented for about two years now. Actually, I think it's just shy of two years. Believe it or not, VIA was on paper requisitions up until 2024. So the ERP system is fairly new. The data doesn't go back very far, and even what we can spit out doesn't do us a whole lot of good in regards to category management efforts. It's hard to understand or put yourself in a good place from a negotiation standpoint or even really identify what your opportunities are for consolidation and synergies without having good data spit out of your system. The data is not that good, or it would just take us too long to sift through all of it and categorize it properly' (discovery call 2026-04-23). He described the manual burden: category managers have spent several months meticulously building a detailed spend taxonomy (category → subcategory → item level), but they cannot populate actual spend into those boxes because the Infor ERP data is too messy and categorization is too labor-intensive. This blocks their ability to execute category management strategy: no visibility into consolidation opportunities, no leverage in supplier negotiations, no data-driven sourcing decisions. He framed the pain as both an operational bottleneck (time and manual effort) and a strategic gap (can't do category management without the data foundation). Pain owner is clear: Brandon Wood (VP Procurement) and his category leads. Compelling event is the transition to category management (a strategic shift Brandon has led at prior companies and is now implementing at VIA), but there is no forcing-function date (no contract expiration, no regulatory deadline, no budget window closing imminently beyond the FY27 flat-budget constraint). Pain is real and acknowledged, but urgency is low (no crisis, just a capability gap).

Brandon Wood briefly mentioned that VIA can justify sole-source vendor selection under FTA rules if the scope uniquely fits one vendor ('we can justify a sole source... if we figure out that it doesn't really make much sense to go to anybody else'), but no details on MSA, SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement approval mechanics were covered. No Current_Contract_Expiration__c date on record. No mention of redlining, compliance review, ERP integration scoping, or implementation partner involvement (e.g., Infor SI). The deal is at early discovery stage (Qualified) with no commercial or contracting discussions yet. Gap: paper process is entirely unknown — no MSA/SOW template shared, no InfoSec review scoped, no procurement/legal approval chain documented, no contracting timeline. Next move: once the business case and EB validation are secured, propose a parallel InfoSec and legal review track (send standard MSA/Order Form + InfoSec questionnaire) and map VIA's procurement approval flow (who signs, who reviews, typical cycle time for $70k+ software buys under FTA rules).

economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is identified but not engaged. Brandon Wood (VP Procurement) named the CFO role explicitly in context of budget authority: when asked about invoice volume and platform cost ($70-140k/year estimate), he deferred and stated the decision would require VP-level sign-off across IT, Finance, and Operations, and noted the CEO mandated a flat FY27 budget to the entire executive management team (EMT) the week before the call. He said: 'My CEO basically told all of EMT to keep 2027 flat. So it's really not a whole lot of meat on the bone for me to be able to bring in an expensive AI platform right now... maybe we can implement some stuff the AI platform does enough that it can affect multiple different divisions in a positive way and provide an ROI across multiple divisions that we can justify' (discovery call 2026-04-23). This explicitly positions the CFO and/or the cross-divisional executive layer (IT VP, Finance VP, Operations VP, possibly CEO for large platform buys) as the budget gatekeepers. However, NONE of these power buyers have been engaged: no CFO call, no IT VP call, no Finance VP meeting. All engagement to date is with Brandon (VP Procurement, the deal sponsor but not the final budget authority) and Brian (Director, a user/influencer). The EB is known by role but untouched, and the buyer himself acknowledged this is 'not a one, two, or even 3 call conversation' and requires internal circulation to IT/Finance/Ops VPs before any decision. Gap: no direct EB engagement, no validated budget, no executive-level sponsorship. Status is passive (Brandon must sell internally) vs active (EB driving the buy). Next move: demand a joint call with Brandon and the Finance VP or CFO to validate FY27 budget feasibility and quantify ROI across divisions, and map the EMT decision process.

decision_process.narrative: Buyer described an informal, multi-stage internal evaluation process but no formal procurement or vendor selection timeline. Brandon Wood stated the next steps as: (1) VIA will circulate the Zycus recording and materials internally to the VPs of IT, Finance, and Operations, (2) internal evaluation to assess fit, budget feasibility, and existing system overlap, (3) follow-up discussion with Zycus in 2-3 weeks (early-to-mid May 2026, now overdue by 5-6 weeks) to review feedback and alignment. He acknowledged: 'I'm in no way the start and end of this decision' and 'We're meeting with a couple other AI firms', signaling a multi-vendor evaluation without a formal RFP. He also noted that for purchases over $10,000 VIA must run a solicitation (RFP/RFQ) under FTA rules, but there are cases where they can justify a sole-source award if the scope matches a single vendor uniquely ('if we figure out our scope and there's a platform that matches that scope exactly and it wouldn't make much sense to go to anybody else, then we're pretty much justified in a sole source'). No timeline for internal evaluation completion, no date for vendor shortlist or RFP kickoff, no procurement approval chain documented beyond 'VPs of IT, Finance, Operations must sign off'. The approval process for large software buys is implicit (multi-VP + CFO/CEO given the EMT budget mandate) but not mapped. The call sequence shows no escalation or exec join: only Brandon (VP) and Brian (Director) on the discovery call, no follow-up calls scheduled or completed. Gap: no formal decision timeline, no RFP schedule, no mapped approval chain, no scheduled next milestone. Next move: re-engage Brandon with a proposed timeline (discovery recap → fit workshop with IT/Finance VPs in July → business case finalization in Aug → RFP or sole-source justification in Sept for FY27 budget lock), and request a joint call with IT and Finance VPs to formalize the process.

Screen-shared category taxonomy (construction services breakdown) during call
decision_criteria.narrative: Buyer articulated evaluation criteria informally during the discovery call but no formal RFP, scorecard, or written requirements document exists. Brandon Wood stated the evaluation will assess: (1) fit with existing systems (Infor ERP integration, overlap with b2g eSourcing already live), (2) budget feasibility within the flat FY27 constraint, (3) ability to deliver AI-powered spend categorization against VIA's predefined category/subcategory/item-level taxonomy (he screen-shared a detailed construction-services breakdown and said 'It would be helpful to be able to tell the AI platform: take our data and then make it fit into these boxes'), (4) compliance with FTA procurement regulations (he emphasized 'there has to be a human level to keep us out of trouble with the FTA'), and (5) cross-divisional ROI to justify the spend ('affect multiple divisions in a positive way'). He also noted VIA is 'meeting with a couple other AI firms that do not exactly what you guys do but something similar', signaling a multi-vendor comparison without a formal RFP structure. Brian Colbert asked detailed questions about Infor ERP integration and approval workflows, indicating IT/ERP compatibility is a decision gate. No formal BRD, no weighted scorecard, no RFP timeline. The criteria are known from conversation but not documented or formalized. Gap: no written decision criteria, no RFP process, no timeline for vendor down-select. Next move: send a discovery questionnaire capturing their taxonomy, integration requirements, and FTA compliance needs, and propose a fit workshop with IT and Finance to formalize criteria and build the business case.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is strong: buyer is AI Curious trending toward AI Hungry. Brandon Wood proactively framed the problem as an AI use case ('AI... there has to be something that can help us rationalize that data quicker and categorize it based off of our inputs, what we want it to look like. We can tell it what we want it to do, then feed it our data, and it can spit out the categories of spend and maybe even identify the opportunities for us'). He explicitly sought AI-powered automation to solve a manual, time-intensive pain (category managers spending months building taxonomy but unable to populate it with spend data due to poor ERP output). He also articulated a realistic understanding of AI boundaries in a regulated environment ('I don't think AI can be implemented to do everything soup to nuts... there has to be a human level to keep us out of trouble with the FTA'), showing he wants AI augmentation, not full autonomy. His tone was exploratory but serious ('this is all data finding for me... figure out how we could implement AI to make my category team's lives easier and if it's even doable or makes sense from a financial standpoint'). He also volunteered that he is meeting with other AI vendors, reinforcing that AI is a strategic priority for VIA's procurement transformation. However, no AIS (AI excitement) score is on record in Salesforce (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c all null), so the AI fit read comes entirely from the call. Buyer signal: AI Curious (actively exploring AI solutions, understands the use case, wants to validate feasibility and ROI) with potential to become AI Hungry if the business case lands and budget opens. No resistance or skepticism expressed; the constraint is budget, not AI appetite.

competitive_position.summary: VIA is running a multi-vendor evaluation with one active competitor already deployed and others in the mix. Primary current threat: b2g eSourcing platform (high threat, incumbent status, live in production as of mid-April 2026 per buyer statement on 2026-04-23 call). b2g owns the eSourcing module, creating overlap/displacement challenge for Zycus's full Source-to-Pay offering. Buyer's tone was neutral-to-positive on b2g (not dissatisfied, just deployed it, no stated intent to replace), so Zycus must position around b2g (integrate or displace long-term via superior AI + full-platform consolidation). Secondary active threats: unnamed 'couple other AI firms that do not exactly what you guys do but something similar' (medium threat, multi-vendor eval active but no named finalists, eval stage and timeline unknown). Likely procurement-AI or spend-analytics niche players, but unconfirmed. Do-nothing is also a threat (medium): flat FY27 budget, no crisis forcing the buy, buyer can defer if ROI case doesn't land. Infor ERP is the integration anchor (not a competitor, but IT will gate on seamless ERP connectivity). Zycus's wedge: AI-powered Spend Analysis with custom taxonomy fit (the specific capability Brandon requested and screen-shared his taxonomy for) + full Source-to-Pay consolidation play (displace b2g + manual processes long-term) + cross-divisional ROI (Procurement + Finance + IT + Ops) + FTA compliance. Recency-weighted ranking: (1) b2g eSourcing — active, high threat, live incumbent as of April 2026. (2) Unnamed AI vendors — active, medium threat, eval in progress as of April 2026. (3) Do-nothing / defer — medium threat, budget constraint and low urgency. (4) Infor ERP — low threat (integration partner, not competitor, but creates inertia and IT gate).

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong on capability but under-activated. Brandon Wood explicitly sought AI-powered spend categorization ('AI... there has to be something that can help us rationalize that data quicker and maybe even categorize it based off of our inputs') and showed high interest in the Merlin/Spend Analysis AI features Bailey demonstrated. He screen-shared VIA's custom taxonomy and asked how Zycus AI could fit their data into those boxes, which Bailey confirmed is exactly what the platform does. Brandon also emphasized FTA compliance guardrails ('AI can't do everything soup to nuts, there has to be a human level to keep us out of trouble'), which aligns with Zycus's approval-workflow design. However, Bailey did not deeply anchor the Merlin/agentic-AI story or differentiate Zycus's AI maturity (using AI since 2016, organically grown vs. bolt-on) beyond a brief mention. The call focused on functional fit (taxonomy, ERP integration, modules) more than AI wedge vs. competitors. Brandon is also evaluating 'a couple other AI firms', so Zycus has not locked in AI thought-leadership or preference. Products field shows 'Merlin' (correct — Spend Analysis is the Merlin AI module), but the AI narrative was not the primary differentiator on the call. Under-positioned risk: medium — buyer wants AI and Zycus has it, but the AI wedge vs. competitors was not sharpened, and the agentic/autonomous-negotiation story (a Merlin flagship) was covered briefly without deep buyer engagement on it. Next move: send a Merlin-specific use case (mock up VIA's taxonomy with AI-categorized spend output, show the autonomous categorization in action) and a one-pager on Zycus AI maturity + FTA compliance guardrails to differentiate from the 'other AI firms' and anchor Zycus as the AI leader in procurement.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Buyer's stated needs align well with Zycus Spend Analysis and full Source-to-Pay capabilities, but fit is only partially validated (single discovery call, no hands-on demo or POC, no IT/Finance VP validation, no formal BRD). Key alignment: (1) AI-powered spend categorization against custom taxonomy — Brandon explicitly requested this and screen-shared VIA's detailed category structure, saying 'It would be helpful to be able to tell the AI platform: take our data and then make it fit into these boxes'; Zycus Spend Analysis can deliver this. (2) Infor ERP integration — Brian asked multiple detailed questions about ERP connectivity and approval workflows; Zycus integrates with Infor (1,200+ integration touchpoints per Bailey on call). (3) FTA compliance guardrails — Brandon emphasized 'there has to be a human level to keep us out of trouble with the FTA'; Zycus supports approval workflows and audit trails for public-sector compliance. (4) Cross-divisional ROI — Brandon said the platform must 'affect multiple divisions in a positive way and provide an ROI across multiple divisions' to justify the spend; Zycus Source-to-Pay touches Procurement, Finance, IT, and Operations. (5) Phased/modular approach — Brandon acknowledged budget constraint and said 'maybe we can implement some stuff' if it makes sense; Zycus offers standalone Spend Analysis as Phase 1 entry with option to expand. Gaps/exposures: (1) b2g eSourcing overlap — Zycus eSourcing module overlaps with b2g, which is already live; buyer did not express dissatisfaction with b2g, so displacing it is not an immediate need (Zycus must position as long-term consolidation play, not urgent replacement). (2) Cost sensitivity — buyer stated flat FY27 budget and 'not a whole lot of meat on the bone'; $70-140k/year estimate for Spend Analysis alone may be a stretch without proven ROI. (3) IT/InfoSec validation unknown — no IT VP or InfoSec engagement yet; Infor integration and data security will be gates. (4) Multi-vendor eval in progress — buyer is meeting with other AI vendors; Zycus has not differentiated or locked in preferred-vendor status. Overall fit: aligned on capability, exposed on budget/timing and competitive positioning.
